title: Automate Your Money
summary: Automating your savings and bills removes the need for willpower every month. A simple system that pays yourself first helps money habits run on autopilot.

sections.heading: Pay Yourself First
sections.body: Paying yourself first means setting aside savings as soon as your salary arrives, before you spend on anything else. This flips the usual habit of saving only whatever is left over, which is often nothing. When savings come off the top automatically, you adjust your spending to what remains.

sections.heading: Automate SIPs and Savings
sections.body: A Systematic Investment Plan (SIP) lets a fixed amount move into an investment on a set date each month without you having to act. Automating transfers to savings or investments on payday makes the habit consistent and removes the temptation to skip a month. The point here is the system, not any particular fund or product.

sections.heading: Automate Bills and Review
sections.body: Setting up auto-payments for recurring bills like rent, electricity and subscriptions helps you avoid late fees and missed dates. Once your system is running, a short monthly review is enough to check that everything went through and nothing is wasteful. A simple, automated system frees your attention for bigger decisions.

options: Save before spending on anything else
Spend first and save what is left
Pay all your friends back first
A fixed amount invested automatically on a set date each month
To keep the saving habit consistent and avoid skipping

quiz.prompt: A benefit of auto-paying recurring bills is:
options: Avoiding late fees and missed due dates
Removing the need to ever check your account

quiz.prompt: Even with automation, what is still useful to do?
options: Do a short monthly review of your system

disclaimer: Educational content only. This is a simulation, not financial advice.
